data.title: WidePoint's Valuation Disconnect Looks Like An Entry Signal
data.description:
Thesis

WidePoint Corporation (NYSE: NYSE:WYY) is a federally focused IT services firm specializing in secure mobile management, identity authentication, and telecom lifecycle solutions for high-compliance environments.

From my perspective, despite the stock trading down sharply in the past month, and in the wake of its latest earnings release, the market is overlooking a company that is quietly transitioning from operational stabilization to scalable growth. With improving cash flows, deep federal contract visibility, and strategic positioning in a high-barrier ecosystem, I see this pullback not as a sign of weakness, but as a potential inflection point for a long-underappreciated company in government tech infrastructure.

Profitability Within Reach

WidePoint is one of those oddly compelling contradictions you sometimes find in the federal tech world, a company that, by all appearances, is getting stronger in exactly the ways you'd hope, but whose route to actually turning a profit still seems to hover just beyond reach.

That's not meant as a criticism, but more a nod to the dilemma that quite a few investors seem to be wrestling with. How do you go about valuing a company that's got strong revenue growth momentum since 2021, long-term contract clarity, and a decent strategic foothold, but still ends up losing money? In WidePoint's case, the answer seems to be leaning more toward the optimistic side, even if a few warning signs haven't entirely gone away.

Let's begin with what's going right. WidePoint's fourth-quarter and full-year 2024 results delivered something you don't see all that often in the small-cap tech world these days: positive surprises that actually have some momentum behind them. Annual revenue jumped 35% to $142.6 million, coming in ahead of both the company's own guidance and what analysts had expected.

Adjusted EBITDA jumped 229% to $2.6 million, and free cash flow swung from a loss of $300,000 to a gain of $2.5 million, a shift of 933%. The company has now posted positive free cash flow for five quarters in a row, and positive adjusted EBITDA for thirty straight quarters. That's a pattern.

What's more interesting is where that revenue is actually coming from. It's not the usual burst of SaaS license sales from volatile, venture-backed startups. Instead, it's grounded in long-term federal contracts, with $290 million in backlog at the end of the year, not counting the $25 million Spiral 4 award from a U.S. Department of Defense combat support agency. These aren't the kind of contracts you get from meeting regulatory requirements, knowing the field, and showing you can deliver. WidePoint's recent FedRAMP authorization for its Intelligent Technology Management System (ITMS) is a significant development on that front. It places the company among a small group of providers cleared for high-trust, high-security federal cloud contracts, giving it a solid footing for upcoming opportunities like DHS CWMS 3.0, NASA SEWP VI, and the large-scale Alliant 3 procurement cycle.

Let's talk about what this actually means in practice. Take the DHS CWMS 3.0 recompete, for example. This is the third iteration of a massive wireless services contract from the Department of Homeland Security. It covers everything from mobile device lifecycle management to secure communications across one of the most sprawling and sensitive government agencies. DHS needs a vendor with embedded security protocols, track records, and FedRAMP compliance. WidePoint checks those boxes now, and it's already the incumbent.

Then there's SEWP VI, NASA's government-wide IT contract, which comes with an expected ceiling in the tens of billions. It's one of the key vehicles for federal spending on cloud services, cybersecurity, telecom, and endpoint management, and it's used by just about every agency you can name. If WidePoint secures a spot on SEWP VI, it would give the company steady access to pre-approved funding from dozens of departments. And it's worth noting, that only a small group of providers even qualify for it.

Alliant 3, meanwhile, is the crown jewel. It's the GSA's next-generation IT services contract, with a projected ceiling of $75 billion, covering everything from standard IT work to newer fields like AI, machine learning, and quantum computing. If WidePoint is seriously in the running for it, that's a clear sign they've stepped up to a higher tier.

And it's worth considering what this looks like in the broader picture. With companies like Okta (OKTA) and AT&T (T) outperforming the S&P 500 in 2025 and pulling investor focus toward cybersecurity and managed mobility, WidePoint is in that general vicinity, positioned at the crossroads of secure mobile device management, identity infrastructure, and federal compliance. Okta's outperformance reinforces that secure identity isn't optional, it's foundational.

AT&T's run shows that mobile infrastructure is gaining in complexity and budget priority. WidePoint may not match their scale, but it services the granular, compliance-driven, operational layers they can't easily reach.

On the other end of the spectrum, Cognizant's (CTSH) flatlining YTD reflects the vulnerability of generalized IT providers exposed to commercial cycles. WidePoint, in contrast, is leaning into the defensive posture investors are hungry for right now.

Recession Risk Context

That said, it wouldn't be honest to call this a straightforward story. The wider market environment adds more context to where WidePoint fits into all of this. TS Lombard has been warning that recession risks are being underestimated, and with fiscal policy becoming less predictable in the Trump administration's second term, a lot of investors are beginning to pull back from cyclical plays. In that light, WidePoint's contracts, like that newly secured Spiral 4 deal, stand out as steady, federally backed revenue sources. They're tied to essential infrastructure that keeps running, no matter what the economy is doing.

Meanwhile, the company continues to operate at a GAAP loss, of $1.9 million for the year, or -$0.21 per share, though that's a sharp improvement from 2023's $4 million loss. Q4 came in at -$0.04 per share, which was better than expected but still in the red. That net loss, combined with only a modest cash build, $6.8 million in cash on hand at year-end, flat with 2023, makes it harder for some investors to get comfortable. Unbilled receivables tied to federal customers are being worked through, but it underscores how fragile the working capital dynamics can be for a company of this scale operating in a heavily bureaucratic environment.

Turning to margins, on the surface, gross profit margin dropped to 13% from 15% last year, mostly because carrier services, those useful but not especially lucrative beasts, pumped in $28.6 million in revenue while contributing next to nothing to the bottom line. Take those out of the picture, though, and things start to look a bit more respectable: the core business posted a gross margin of 36% in Q4, up from 32% the year before.

And then there's the matter of guidance, which isn't really there.

Management was clear enough about wanting to hit positive EPS in 2025, but they stopped short of giving actual revenue or EBITDA targets, saying they need more time to update forecasts based on how Q1 is shaping up. Fair enough, but it does leave a void, particularly for institutional investors who like to have something, anything concrete to plug into their models. Meanwhile, even though the federal pipeline looks promising, the ongoing consolidation of procurement under the GSA and some rumblings about cutting back on non-statutory FAR clauses could end up making WidePoint's usual contract routes somewhat bumpier than usual.

Cash Burn and Cash Runway

Let's take a closer look at cash flow. By the end of 2024, WidePoint had just under $7 million in cash on hand. During that time, it generated roughly $1.7 million in free cash flow, which means the company isn't burning through cash, it's actually operating with a positive cash flow. That said, if we conservatively excluded working capital improvements and smoothed for operational volatility, a more neutral baseline free cash flow would suggest minimal net cash consumption at worst.

Still, if WidePoint were to return to a modest monthly cash burn of around $0.3 million, which lines up with earlier periods of net outflows, it would have enough cash for a cash runway to last roughly 22 to 24 months. That estimate doesn't factor in any additional liquidity from its existing $4 million revolving credit line or potential new contract awards.

Even so, it's worth not emphasizing that WidePoint's small market capitalization, just under $25 million, along with its fairly limited daily trading volume, introduces some structural liquidity concerns. These elements, while not uncommon in the microcap space, can make it more difficult for institutional investors to engage meaningfully and can, under the right, or wrong, conditions, lead to sharper price swings.

Valuation and Growth Potential

The company has made it clear that achieving positive EPS in 2025 is a top strategic goal. Consensus analyst estimates support this trajectory, projecting EPS growth of 42.86% in 2025 and 91.67% in 2026.

The free cash flow numbers do a solid job of backing up the optimistic view. Levered free cash flow rose 146.91% year-over-year, which is well ahead of the Information Technology sector's median of 15.29%.

Operating cash flow jumped even more, 168.98% over the year, outpacing sector norms by more than tenfold. And yet, despite all that cash being generated, WidePoint wrapped up 2024 with pretty much the same cash balance it started with: that $6.8 million. The reason? A chunk of that money is still caught up in unbilled receivables and the slow-turning gears of federal contract administration, things the company says it's addressing.

Still, with only $4.94 million in debt and a modest enterprise value of $23.04 million, the company looks financially stable and unlikely to need to raise dilutive capital anytime soon.

Against the broader backdrop of the IT consulting and services industry, forecast to grow at a CAGR of 8.38% through 2030, WidePoint's niche positioning in secure telecom lifecycle management and federal cybersecurity gives it an edge. The IT consulting segment itself is forecast to grow at 13.3% CAGR in 2029, driven by digital transformation, cloud adoption, and enterprise security spending. WidePoint, with its recent FedRAMP authorization and a contract backlog, is in a strong position to capitalize on this tailwind, especially in the high-trust, high-barrier federal procurement space.

According to Seeking Alpha, WidePoint's revenue growth is outpacing the sector by more than 500%, and its forward EBITDA growth is estimated at 57.4%, compared to a sector median of 9.47%. Working capital also climbed 75.71% year-over-year, pointing to a stronger liquidity position that could support further growth.

Final Takeaway

I would rate WidePoint a "Buy". While the company's continued GAAP losses and lack of near-term guidance create hesitation, the strengthening fundamentals, improving cash flow profile, and deep federal contract backlog signal a business nearing a meaningful inflection point. With the stock trading sharply lower pre-market despite operational momentum, I see this dislocation as a tactical opportunity rather than a red flag.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Abandonment Risk And Its Impact On Retirement Savings
data.description:
Retirement savings is a critical financial goal that many individuals strive to achieve throughout their working lives. As people live longer and face increasing economic uncertainties, investors cannot overstate the importance of building a robust retirement portfolio. However, a significant threat to this objective is the concept of "retirement savings abandonment risk." This risk can lead to premature withdrawals and discontinuation of contributions, jeopardizing long-term financial security. Understanding and mitigating this risk is essential for reaching retirement goals.

What is retirement savings abandonment risk?

Retirement savings abandonment risk refers to the likelihood that individuals will stop contributing to their retirement accounts or withdraw funds prematurely. This risk is particularly concerning in volatile market conditions when investors may react emotionally to downturns. Most retirement portfolios are designed to balance market risks, such as interest rate and equity risk, with shortfall and longevity risks—the risk that your savings will be too small to finance your retirement or that you will outlive your savings. While addressing these risks is vital for a successful retirement outcome, it often requires taking on additional market risk. Unfortunately, this can inadvertently introduce abandonment risk.

Risks for retirement savers

Market risk: refers to how investments react to market volatility. It is also synonymous with abandonment risk or the risk that participants will abandon their investment if they feel a negative experience is too severe.

Inflation risk: the risk of reduced purchasing power due to rapidly rising prices. Longevity risk: the risk of an individual outliving their savings, either from a savings shortfall or living longer than planned.

Shortfall risk: the risk of not having enough savings to last throughout retirement.

From Principal’s research, we can observe this behavioral finance phenomenon when investors exit their retirement plans during market downturns, typically opting for safer, cash-like investments at the worst possible time. Abandoning a retirement investment strategy at the market's lowest point can have severe consequences, ultimately jeopardizing an investor's retirement goals. The implications of abandoning a long-term investment strategy can ripple through a retiree's financial life, leading to a loss of potential growth and increased reliance on social safety nets.

Common scenarios where abandonment occurs

At Principal Asset Management, our proprietary research reveals several behavioral and emotional factors that contribute to abandonment risk, including:

  1. Loss aversion: Research shows that investors tend to feel the pain of losses more acutely than the pleasure of equivalent gains. This can lead to rash decisions during market downturns.
  2. Myopic risk aversion: Individuals often focus too heavily on short-term market fluctuations, overshadowing their long-term investment objectives. This is especially true for those nearing or in retirement, who may panic and withdraw during a downturn.
  3. Recency bias: Investors frequently overweight recent market performance, which can skew their decision-making. Investors may be more inclined to abandon their savings plans if the market performs poorly.
  4. Flight to safety reflex: During times of volatility, the emotional response to seek safety in cash-like investments can lead to premature withdrawals, further exacerbating the problem.

What drives abandonment risk?

Studies in behavioral finance indicate that risk tolerance varies with age. Younger investors may be more willing to take risks, but as individuals approach retirement age, their risk tolerance typically decreases. When faced with a market downturn, older investors may abandon their plans for perceived safer investments, increasing their risk of financial shortfalls as they cross into and live in retirement.

Based on our proprietary research, different age groups exhibit varying risk tolerance levels. The risk of abandoning retirement plans increases as individuals approach retirement, peaking at the time of retirement. Historically, we’ve observed a rise in the abandonment of retirement plans during significant market downturns, which is detrimental for participants.

Our projections for future abandonment risk are grounded in a 2.0 to 2.25 standard deviation shift alongside our updated annual portfolio risk and return assessments. For younger investors (ages 25-50), we identify a critical threshold at a -25.9% annualized return over two years. For those nearing retirement (ages 50-65), this threshold is -13.5% over the same period. Finally, for retirees (ages 65 and older), we consider a -9.0% annualized return over two years as the critical point.

Once investors have abandoned their retirement plans in favor of safer investments, re-entering the market can be psychologically and emotionally challenging. That’s especially true after a missed market rebound, which may create a cycle of lost opportunities and reduced growth potential

Abandonment risk often leads to a "buy high, sell low" scenario, which can dramatically alter the trajectory of retirement outcomes. If investors abandon their plans at the wrong time, they may face increased shortfall and longevity risks, significantly impacting their quality of life during retirement. Asset allocators must consider these factors to provide retirement investors the best chance for success.

Understanding how abandonment risk affects retirees differently

Research indicates that abandonment risk varies across age groups. Studies show that younger investors are more likely to remain invested during downturns, while older investors are more prone to withdraw funds. Understanding these dynamics is essential for developing targeted education and strategies to mitigate this risk effectively.

  • Younger retirement savers: Generally, younger investors have a higher risk tolerance and can withstand large market drawdowns. Their invested capital is typically smaller, and their human capital (the present value of future earnings) is much higher. This means they have time on their side to recover from market fluctuations.

Risk tolerance changes over time

Investor stage

Abandonment risk assumption*

Early accumulating (Age 25-50)

-26%

Approaching retirement (Age 51-65)

-14%

In retirement (Age 65+)

-9%
Source: Principal Asset Allocation. For illustrative purposes only. *Based on annualized returns and our modeling assumptions. Our expectations for future abandonment are based on a 2.00 to 2.25 standard deviation move coupled with our blended ex-ante portfolio risk and return assumptions, updated annually. For early accumulating investors (approximately ages 25-50) we have assumed that “pain point” to be at -25.9% annualized portfolio return over a two-year period. For the investors approaching retirement (approximately ages 50-65) we have assumed the threshold to be a -13.5% two-year annualized return. And for those in retirement (approximately ages 65+), we assumed a -9.0% two-year annualized return. Intended to be reflective of the views and opinions of the investment manager. The investment manager’s investment philosophy and strategy may not perform as intended and could result in loss or gain. See Important Information for additional details.
  • Employees nearing or in retirement: Conversely, individuals nearing retirement, particularly those already retired, exhibit much lower risk tolerances. For someone in retirement, even a drawdown of over 9% can result in many retirees abandoning their investment plans at precisely the wrong time. The consequences can be devastating, substantially reducing their retirement income and overall quality of life.

Behavioral finance plays a crucial role here. Psychologically, it may be challenging for individuals who have abandoned their retirement plans to re-enter the market after it rebounds. This creates a situation where they can miss out on potential gains, resulting in permanent losses and further exacerbating their financial challenges.

The impact of abandonment risk on retirement savings

The consequences of abandonment risk on retirement savings can be profound and multifaceted:

  1. Loss of long-term growth: One of the most significant impacts of abandonment risk is the loss of long-term growth potential. Abandoning contributions can severely hinder the compounding effect of investments. For instance, during the COVID-19 pandemic, those who remained invested experienced substantial returns compared to those who exited the market. Investors who withdrew during the downturn may have missed the subsequent recovery, leading to a significant opportunity cost.

2. Early withdrawal penalties: Accessing retirement funds prematurely often incurs tax penalties and fees, further diminishing savings. These costs can compound over time, resulting in a significantly reduced nest egg for retirement.

3. Reduced quality of life in retirement: Financial insecurity in later years can increase reliance on social safety nets or family support. This affects the individual's quality of life and can burden family members and society.

Strategies to mitigate retirement savings abandonment risk

Mitigating retirement savings abandonment risk requires a multifaceted approach:

Employers can significantly support retirement savings by implementing auto-re-enrollment in retirement plans, thereby proactively including employees. A welcoming and user-friendly experience is crucial, as it can help individuals easily navigate their retirement options. Additionally, incorporating behavioral nudges—such as reminders and progress tracking—can encourage consistent contributions and foster a sense of commitment. Encouragement and practical tools will empower employees to actively engage with their retirement goals.

  • Professional asset management: Engaging with asset managers who understand the complexities of retirement investing can help mitigate abandonment risk. These professionals can design portfolios that account for market volatility, mitigate risks associated with retirement investing, and keep investors on track with their long-term goals.
  • Education, awareness, and employer support: To address abandonment risk in retirement planning, it is essential to implement financial literacy programs that highlight the importance of consistent saving and provide tools for visualizing long-term goals. Understanding the effects of market fluctuations can help investors stay committed during downturns.
    • Employers can significantly support retirement savings by implementing auto-re-enrollment in retirement plans, thereby proactively including employees. A welcoming and user-friendly experience is crucial, as it can help individuals easily navigate their retirement options. Additionally, incorporating behavioral nudges—such as reminders and progress tracking—can encourage consistent contributions and foster a sense of commitment. Encouragement and practical tools will empower employees to actively engage with their retirement goals.
  • Emergency funds: Encouraging the establishment of separate savings accounts for short-term financial needs can reduce the likelihood of withdrawals from retirement accounts. A financial cushion can provide peace of mind and lessen the urgency to access retirement savings during emergencies.

The significance of tackling abandonment risk

Addressing retirement savings abandonment risk is a critical component of long-term financial planning, and financial professionals play a central role in helping investors manage their retirement strategies. This is particularly true during periods of market stress, which have been shown to disrupt contribution and investing behavior. In fact, left unaddressed, abandonment risk can lead to adverse outcomes not only for individual's retirement, but also for the broader economy, as reliance on public resources grows with an aging U.S. population. Recognizing the structural and behavioral drivers of abandonment risk, and implementing strategies to help retirees stay on track, can make all the difference in delivering successful retirement outcomes.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: United Rentals: Why I Bought It Instead Of Caterpillar (Q1 2025 Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Introduction

A few days ago, when the Trump administration first announced the new import tariffs, I wrote a piece comparing Caterpillar Inc. (CAT) and Deere & Company (DE), in which I showed why the former is currently a more attractive investment than the latter. I was about to pull the trigger and initiate a position in Caterpillar when I realized that I could have a better and safer opportunity to invest in construction without being hit as much by tariffs. In short, I bought United Rentals, Inc. (NYSE:URI) at around $560, and I will explain in this article why I made this choice, after having identified it as a possible buy for my portfolio back in January 2024, when the stock was trading at $633 with strong momentum that brought it close to $875. During that price surge, I monitored the stock and considered the valuation a bit demanding, especially in the case of below-average manufacturing activity. Now, I see the stock as much more attractive, even though the macroeconomic outlook may have worsened.

United Rentals: The Company

United Rentals is headquartered in Stamford, CT, and was founded in 1997. Since then, it has quickly become the world’s largest equipment rental provider.

Today, the company offers a fleet of equipment for rent with a total original cost of $21.43B and generated FY'24 revenues of $15.3B with a sumptuous adj. EBITDA of $7.16B (46.7% margin on sales).

The shareholder structure is as follows. Around 55% of the company's stock is owned by institutional investors, a small percentage (0.36%) is owned by insiders, and 25% is owned by others. Among institutional investors, we have Vanguard Fiduciary Trust Co. with a bit more than 11%, BlackRock Advisors LLC with 6%, State Street Corporation with 4.7%, and Capital Research & Management Co. (Global Investors) with 4.4%.

By the way, BlackRock is also invested in United Rentals' major competitors. It owns a 6.1% stake in Sunbelt Rentals, #2 in the USA, and, not surprisingly, it is also the main shareholder of Herc Rental - the third-largest player in the U.S., with 7.7% of the pie.

Just as a quick comment, it seems institutional investors like this business.

The company presents itself with a concise but very clear Investor Presentation. When I see these kinds of presentations, I am often inclined to think that behind them, we usually may find a strategy-focused and clean mindset able to explain to others what the company is doing, and the criteria used to manage the business.

The following slide gives, in a synthetic way, some important information about this company:

How has URI reached its dominant position? The following picture shows the road map of the strategies adopted since its foundation. In short, the company has executed over 250 strategic acquisitions in North America through 2008, and it has then focused on improving its financial discipline and has expanded into the new specialty category, offering adjacent product lines and developing services and solutions. The strategy is clear. At first, the company fueled its growth and then rationalized its operations, maintaining a growth attitude mainly through M&A activity.

United Rentals' business is highly concentrated in the U.S. (94% of its branches are here); it has a differentiated mix of products to be supplied to its customers, covering the majority of the needs in the construction and maintenance business. It owns a fleet of over 1M units, such as forklifts, trucks, or even compressors.

Its diversification is well-structured through contiguous sectors. This is important because it enables United Rentals to run one coherent business, without depending too heavily on any single area of its operations. This is clearly shown by its customer mix: 46% is non-residential construction, 49% is industrial, and only 5% is residential construction. This can help us deconstruct the bias that renting construction equipment is a business strictly related to home building.

We should also understand why customers prefer renting instead of buying, as this is part of the thesis I developed to pick United Rentals over Caterpillar.

Firstly, customers can preserve capital without needing to fund heavy capital expenditures in machinery. Secondly, renting outsources a lot of labor, such as maintenance, pick-up and delivery; it also helps customers save on storage, and it reduces downtime because United Rentals guarantees customer support 24/7.

United Rentals, of course, benefits from these advantages and can charge a fee that more than covers its fleet investment. Moreover, United Rentals also sells its used equipment and usually makes money. With new tariffs shielding the U.S. domestic market, used equipment prices will likely go up, and this will boost United Rentals' profits. Usually, around 10% of United Rentals' revenues come from used equipment sales.

United Rentals' Recent Financials

An overview of the 2024 results as well as the 2025 outlook is reported in the following table. The company reported a total revenue of $15.35B and expects its FY25 revenue to be above $15.6B. The adj. EBITDA was $7.16B and should be above $7.2B this year. We should also see the reiteration of another year with FCF above $2B.

Even if Q4 2024 shows a good 9.7% revenue YoY increase, United Rentals forecasts its business to be more or less flat this year. This seems correct, as the current big changes in the trade and diplomatic relationships between the U.S. and other countries don't allow us to predict with a high degree of precision how many businesses will be affected in 2025. As a logical consequence, improvement in profitability will be more related to cost reduction measures rather than volume increases.

United Rentals also reported that its net leverage ratio was 1.8x as of December 31, 2024 (1.6x in 2023). In 2024, it also repurchased $1.5B of common stock and paid dividends worth $434M. More or less, it returned its $2B in FCF to its shareholders. We then heard that the company was pausing its buyback program due to the pending acquisition of H&E. But then, United Rentals' competitor, Herc made a better offer and closed the deal. So, in its press release of February 18, 2025, United Rentals announced that it would immediately restart its share repurchase program, with $250M to be spent.

More in-depth, this is how United Rentals' two segments - general rentals and specialty - performed last year.

General Rentals lost margin in Q4 vs. the same period of the prior year. The same trend was visible for Specialty, but Specialty has a higher margin, which confirms that the company's strategy to invest in this segment (mainly through M&As) is a key driver of its future profits.

This is once again highlighted by United Rentals itself when it explains how Specialty provides strong growth opportunities after having delivered a decade-long CAGR above 20%, growing from only $823M in revenue in 2014 to over $5.1B in revenue at the end of last year.

I have summarized some financial data in the following table, as well as the PE ratio. I added an interesting metric, which is not always found: the capital invested in the fleet and its ratio compared to the companies' revenues.

2024 Revenue YoY Revenue Increase 2024 EBITDA EBITDA margin FCF Fleet Value Fleet Value/Revenues P/E
URI $15.3B 7.1% $7.1B 46.7% $2B $21B 137% 13.3
Sunbelt Rentals $10.8B 12% 4.9% 45.4% $0.2B $17.7B 164% 15
Herc $3.6B 9% $1.58B 44.4% $.03B $7B 194% 9

We see that United Rentals has the advantage of owning the largest market share. This shows that United Rentals has more capital efficiency, and this also suggests that United Rentals' financial leverage and operating efficiency are better than its two main peers. Let's translate it into something investors look at: the lower the fleet value/revenue ratio is, the higher the free cash flow is. Quite important.

United Rentals' Earnings Preview

United Rentals is correctly working to improve its future financial performance, both increasing sales and customer satisfaction, as well as reducing costs. As we have seen, in 2025, the cost-cutting activity will be predominant, and this will be the first thing I will look at when the next earnings report is released. We need to see that costs decrease or don't increase at the same pace as revenues.

If the Trump administration really wants to reshore manufacturing activities in the U.S., this will be a major tailwind for United Rentals because new factories and new plants will need to be built. United Rentals will be on the frontline to service these needs. But before this trend plays out, we might have to wait a couple of quarters until the new tariffs are fully unveiled, and the economic environment becomes clearer. This will enable many companies to understand where they will have to invest in the U.S.

I therefore don't expect Q1 to be particularly affected by the changing environment, and I think United Rentals will probably report a top line more or less in line with its forecast. Currently, analysts are expecting a 3.6% YoY growth to $3.6B, which, I think, is reasonable. My forecast is around $3.5 because some uncertainty might have paused or postponed some projects that were to begin in Q1 2025. More important will be what United Rentals reports as its EPS. The number to watch for is $9.00. If the company beats it, then we will have further proof that cost management is effective and earnings-fattening. If EPS comes in below $8.85, we will have a different situation, and we will have to hear from management what is going on.

United Rentals' business is attractive, with all the main players reporting very good EBITDA margins, showing a stable market where the largest players don't fight on price. Further improvements will be possible with organic and inorganic growth.

With a reasonable P/E and the culture to give money back to its shareholders both via dividends and share repurchases, United Rentals is the best pick in this industry.

Why United Rentals Over Caterpillar

Now, I can go back to the introduction and explain why I picked United Rentals instead of Caterpillar. Don't get me wrong, I consider the latter a fantastic company. Caterpillar has around 50% of its manufacturing activity in the U.S., so I think it could still be protected by some tariffs. But its business, though it earns over 30% of its revenues from services, is more linked to direct machinery sales. These are big investments that customers are unwilling to undertake when the macroeconomic environment is not clear. In times of uncertainty, renting is far more convenient. Moreover, United Rentals' profits will improve as I expect second-hand machinery prices to increase.

It seems the market agrees, and URI has more than doubled CAT's returns in the past decade.

United Rentals' factor grades are also a tier higher than Caterpillar's. In particular, it is still expected to grow moderately, and this is reflected in its EPS revisions grade. Caterpillar beats United Rentals when considering the dividend, with a higher starting yield. But United Rentals more than compensates through its share price track record.

When we consider their valuation, they trade at similar earnings multiple, but their PEG is much different: 1.1 for URI and 2.5 for CAT.

Conclusion

United Rentals is unlikely to triple in one year, but it is a solid company that keeps returning money to its shareholders thanks to its dominant position in the market and geographical proximity to customers. Compared to Caterpillar, it seems more entrenched in the U.S. economy and more protected from the sudden impact of tariffs. Moreover, it could benefit from higher used equipment sales.

data.title: Eli Lilly: Cementing Its GLP-1 Empire With Orforglipron's Promising Phase 3 Results
data.description:
Overview

Today, Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) revealed Phase 3 data for its oral GLP-1 receptor agonist, orforglipron. After review, I believe it firmly establishes Eli Lilly as a leader in the oral weight loss market, complementing its injectable GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist, Zepbound.

Orforglipron's Phase 3 Results Strengthen Eli Lilly's Position in the Competitive GLP-1 Landscape

Eli Lilly’s ACHIEVE-1 was the “first of seven Phase 3 studies” evaluating orforglipron in obesity and diabetes populations. This particular study focused on 559 participants with type 2 diabetes who are also overweight. So, the primary outcome was actually hemoglobin A1C reduction.

After scrolling through social media, I’ve already seen others misunderstand the weight loss results. Patients taking the highest dose (36 mg) lost 7.9% of their weight after 40 weeks. This may not seem like a lot, but we must consider the population. This was no traditional weight loss study. First, the participants just had to be “overweight” (≥23 kg/m2). As displayed in the table above, the baseline weight was 90.2 kg. This pales in comparison to traditional studies in obese participants. For instance, Eli Lilly’s Phase 3 (Study 1) trial for Zepbound saw its participants average a body weight of ~105 kg. In a Phase 2 trial (published in NEJM), daily orforglipron resulted in up to 12.3% weight loss in the 36 mg arm. In this study, the baseline mean weight was close to 109 kg. So, do not scoff at 7.9% weight loss in ACHIEVE-1.

What was most important about ACHIEVE-1 results was not the efficacy outcomes, but the tolerability ones. Here, we saw that orforglipron safety data was comparable with other GLP-1s (diarrhea, dyspepsia, nausea, vomiting). The discontinuation rate due to adverse events was just 8% in the 36 mg arm. Critically, there were no liver safety signals. This is especially relevant after Pfizer (PFE) axed its own oral GLP-1 receptor agonist, danuglipron, earlier this week after observing a potential drug-induced liver injury.

Another important consideration about orforglipron is that it is a small molecule. Relative to large peptide molecules like semaglutide or tirzepatide (Zepbound), small molecules are typically easier and less costly to manufacture. This should help avoid the supply constraints that capped injectable GLP-1s last year. In fact, Eli Lilly already has a stockpile of orforglipron worth $548 million ahead of its anticipated 2026 launch in obesity. This has been estimated to support sales up to $10 billion. Moreover, Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) oral semaglutide (approved as Rybelsus for diabetes) has food and water restrictions that complicate its administration. Oral semaglutide is anticipated to add an obesity indication within the next year.

Below is a look at some of the most advanced oral obesity drugs. This is not intended to be a comprehensive list.

Next on tap for Eli Lilly is data from the Phase 3 ATTAIN program in obesity. Analysts are anticipating weight loss in the range of ~15%. I, personally, think anything over 12% will suffice. The difference in weight loss between oral semaglutide and orforglipron would likely be statistically negligible at that delta and the latter’s administration advantage is a major selling point. As for market expectations, Eli Lilly’s orforglipron is projected to eclipse $8 billion in peak annual revenue. I believe this is a reasonable expectation. In sum, orforglipron is expected to quickly become a major blockbuster for Eli Lilly.

FY2024 and Q4 Earnings

In Q4 and full year 2024, Eli Lilly reported revenue of approximately $13.5 billion and $45 billion (up 32% year-over-year), respectively. This growth was predominantly driven by its incretin products, Mounjaro (diabetes) and Zepbound. Despite being approved in November 2023, Zepbound generated a whopping $1.9 billion in Q4 2024 revenue, gaining momentum from a recent trial that proved its superiority in weight loss outcomes versus Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy.

Eli Lilly is diversified beyond obesity and diabetes. For instance, Verzenio (breast cancer) grew 36% year-over-year to reach $1.555 billion in Q4. In addition, Taltz (plaque psoriasis) grew 21% year-over-year to reach $952 million in Q4.

Non-GAAP net income in Q4 was $4.8 billion, a 114% increase over the same period in 2023.

FY2025 and Q1 Outlook

As for Eli Lilly’s 2025 outlook, it guided for full-year revenues between $58 billion and $61 billion, representing 32% growth at the midpoint.

The company is expected to report its Q1 2025 earnings on May 1, 2025. Analysts are expecting $12.77 billion in revenue and an EPS of $4.70. This implies significant growth compared to Q1 2024 (revenue $8.77 billion). Notably, it also implies only about 21.5% of the midpoint for full-year revenue guidance, suggesting that analysts expect a substantial acceleration in revenue as the year progresses. The company has been working to expand internationally and increase production. Investors should keep a close eye on Eli Lilly’s non-incretin portfolio performance, continued U.S. uptake of Zepbound and Mounjaro, and Eli Lilly's manufacturing capacity ahead of orforglipron’s launch.

Financial Health

As of December 31, Eli Lilly had $3.268 billion in cash and equivalents. Short-term investments were $154.8 million. Total current assets were $32.739 billion, while total current liabilities were $28.376 billion. So, Eli Lilly appears to have solid liquidity for the next twelve months. Long-term debt was $28.644 billion.

Given that Eli Lilly generated $8.818 billion from operations in the past twelve months, its debt obligations do not appear overwhelming.

LLY Stock Valuation Assumptions

I conducted a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis to get an idea of what expectations are implied by Eli Lilly’s valuation.

As for inputs, $61 billion in Year 1 revenue represents Eli Lilly’s higher estimate for 2025. 15% revenue growth appears feasible in light of the company’s leadership position in a booming obesity treatment market. 25% FCF margins are achievable for mature pharmaceutical companies. Notably, Eli Lilly made substantial capital expenditure investments in 2024 (e.g., manufacturing) that limited its FCF margin, but I expect this is a temporary bug and that Eli Lilly’s FCF should normalize. The 0.73 beta (24-month) is according to Seeking Alpha data. This varies among sources and timeframes, but I felt that 0.73 was appropriate for a mature pharmaceutical company. Cost of debt and tax rate are estimates based on public information. These are impossible to know for certain because the values are not inherently fixed and are subject to economic, regulatory, and company-specific factors. In any event, Eli Lilly’s discount rate of 7.77% is predominately equity-driven (CAPM).

Based on these assumptions, which I believe to be both optimistic and reasonable, Eli Lilly appears overvalued by nearly 13%.

Next, I conduct a sensitivity analysis that also acts like a “reverse DCF” in that it reveals what market expectations are implied by Eli Lilly’s valuation.

Eli Lilly appears to be priced for 17% revenue growth (assuming all other things remain the same). This is totally within reason. As you can see, Eli Lilly is very sensitive to growth % changes, which is why its stock is up over 10% after orforglipron data. On the other hand, any setbacks (e.g., postmarketing Zepbound safety concerns) could significantly derail its valuation.

Finally, I conduct a Monte Carlo simulation to account for variability in key inputs like cost of debt, terminal growth rate, revenue growth, and margins. This provides a more realistic and dynamic view of possible share price outcomes.

As you can see, Eli Lilly appears reasonably priced (between the 5th and 95th percentiles) for my base assumptions. Notably, that 5th percentile price of $467.29 should act as a “floor” to LLY, while the 95th percentile price of $1,292.52 should act as a ceiling (unless there is a significant change to any key assumptions). In any event, the risk/reward profile appears balanced at current prices if you believe Eli Lilly is capable of 15% growth and 25% FCF margins over the next nine years.

Conclusion

Today’s ACHIEVE-1 results help cement Eli Lilly as a leader in the oral and injectable weight loss treatment landscape. I believe that orforglipron now sets a high benchmark for other GLP-1 candidates in development. ATTAIN obesity trial data will be key. The safety needs to hold up and the market has high expectations for weight loss outcomes. Critically, the company appears more than prepared for a 2026 launch and orforglipron could feasibly generate billions each year in revenue.

The usual risk factors persist. Eli Lilly remains primarily dependent on one drug (tirzepatide) and any setbacks (i.e. postmarketing safety revelations) would send its stock spiraling. Moreover, competition in the obesity treatment market is fierce. Existing drugs like Wegovy and future orals and injectables will compete with Eli Lilly’s offerings. Finally, Eli Lilly’s stock is priced for significant optimism regarding its growth prospects, so flawless execution across all facets of its business will be necessary to sustain momentum.

Since my last look in July 2024, which focused on its Alzheimer’s asset, Eli Lilly’s stock has declined 7% (in-line with the broader market) despite positive developments (e.g., Zepbound market performance, Phase 3 orforglipron data). All in all, I like Eli Lilly’s outlook and its current price is very tolerable, meriting another Buy rating (outperform).

data.title: TKO Group: WWE Set For New Type Of WrestleMania Battle With Pricing
data.description:
It’s been a wild few years for WWE that has come with its share of highs and lows. However, with Vince McMahon out of the picture, the TKO Group Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:TKO) acquisition a few years old, and a new leadership team firmly in place, as we again reach one of the company’s annual marquee moments, many investors may be compelled to inquire more about the long game.

Yes, you can look at the numbers and see a company solidly trading in triple digits, but as I’ve said many times (across many companies) the numbers don’t always tell the full story. For example, this weekend is WrestleMania – the pinnacle of sports entertainment – coming to you live from Las Vegas. On paper, this is the company’s time to shine, but something this year feels a bit off.

Despite all the glitz and glamour, this year’s “showcase of the immortals” seems surprisingly more “mortal” than usual. Is that really the case or will it all come together when the first bell rings?

First as always, some background.

I’d imagine the majority of you reading this understand the top-level aspects of a company like WWE. But for those who may be new to this or looking for a refresher, WrestleMania is the Super Bowl of professional wrestling. It’s the culmination of its big storylines from the past few months and sets the stage for the future.

Legends are made, streaks are broken and new superstars emerge.

Or that’s how it is supposed to be.

Last year’s 40th anniversary was a WrestleMania for the record books. It was one of the more stacked cards in recent years and overall, extremely well-executed with some truly flawless matches.

Its main event featured Roman Reigns, a champion who at the time had held the title for longer than many in the company’s history, against Cody Rhodes, the second-generation “next big thing,” who couldn’t be more beloved by the fan base. It was also a re-match of the year’s prior main event that ended in upsetting fashion when Reigns retained.

Although in the grand scheme it was the right move for Rhodes to lose when he did because it made this moment mean more when he finally “finished the story” and earned the title that once was worn by his father Dusty “The American Dream” Rhodes – an even more beloved wrestling legend.

Wrestling is pageantry – nobody is denying that.

It’s pre-determined and choreographed, but it’s a misnomer to say it is fake. The pain these men and women put their bodies through for their craft is very real as is the emotion they elicit from the fans.

WWE works best when its audience feels invested in the action. They know the outcome has been set, but they don’t care because they ultimately don’t know the result until they see it happen live.

Yet, this year feels somewhat off.

Maybe it’s the main event feeling a bit forced (Rhodes vs. longtime WWE company man John Cena on his retirement tour), maybe it’s the overall card feeling under-whelming, or maybe it’s the fact that the fans feel priced out of attending.

Take your pick.

I’m not going to go deep into the storyline/narrative concerns because investors don’t really need to know the specifics there, but they do need to know the vibes are different. It’s not that these matches will be bad… the talent involved is too good to deliver substandard results on this stage, but it just feels different.

Maybe it’s because we are coming off a milestone year that was so well-received… that is a possibility. However, now combine those vibes with the fact a large part of that the chatter in recent weeks is around the high price of admission, and you can see another real storyline emerge that hasn’t been as widely discussed in past years.

As Sporting News wrote the other week:

“WrestleMania 41 is a mere one week away, but rising ticket prices may have fans as far away from being in attendance as they've ever been. WWE will gross around $41 million in attendance figures, based on bringing in almost 100,000 paying fans across the events two days. Things are much different than they were almost 20 years ago.”

In fact, with just a short period of time until the big show a larger-than-usual number of tickets were still available. If estimates had held up then WrestleMania 41 would have run the risk of being on track to be the lowest-attended event of its kind since WrestleMania 22.

Yes, WWE will still find a way to say “it broke records,” which is a trademark moment for them, but real fans and bottom-line investors may see through it because the core numbers that lead up to the show have been very clear.

While in the time since that piece posted it does look like more tickets have been sold (as you’d expect in the run-up) – that’s also only after WWE slashed ticket prices by 25%. This is also after a lot of online complaints about the cost and how for some the amount was a non-starter.

The pricing problem should be a concern for WWE and parent company TKO because this is a fan-driven business – and as WWE knows, its fans have no problem being vocal and also have a long memory.

Complicating things is the model the company is moving towards. Earlier this year TKO revealed plans for a series of “city takeovers.” In other words, one multi-day period where WWE, UFC, and PBR (Professional Bull Riding), all three of TKO’s big brands would take over a city with back-to-back shows and events as well as luxury plus-up opportunities.

This goes hand-in-hand with a concept WWE rolled out recently where it essentially set up a residency deal of sorts with one city that would include multiple of its major main events over an extended period of time. The debut city was Indianapolis and the deal kicked off with the 2025 Royal Rumble and will eventually include SummerSlam and WrestleMania – effectively putting three out of the four big WWE annual events in the same location… a first for the company.

The idea behind both approaches is to immerse the TKO brands into specific cities so they stay fans long after the media circus has left town. Now though, instead of one or two smaller scale events coming per year, these cities become the center of these fan’s universe either for a longer period or at a higher level of frequency.

But will the higher price tags that come with it end up causing the opposite effect?

While the events will not always carry WrestleMania pricing they are still not going to be cheap – especially when you account for other spending on merchandise, food and transportation. In other words, you can bring three big similar demo-skewing events to town, but can people afford to attend them? Or more than one of them?

It really is too soon to tell and to WWE and TKO’s credit its new look-leadership has been successful in pivoting where needed. However, as far as WWE is concerned, one can just look to Netflix’s “Monday Night RAW” deal to see despite a global boost via the streamer, WWE’s audience could actually be more finite than once thought.

Despite a strong debut with Netflix, in recent months those numbers quickly came back to Earth. As we’ve seen there’s traditionally been more smoke and mirrors with Netflix’s “numbers,” but there is enough data (both first and third-party centric) to show it is possible RAW’s numbers are not doing anything significantly better (or to be fair, worse) than it was on cable via the USA Network.

The point is that in recent years the cost of being a fan in general has increased. Taking out ticket sales for a minute, merchandise continues to be expensive and if you want to watch one of their premium live events (or have access to its library) it means a Peacock subscription and now if you want to watch RAW it means a Netflix subscription.

Yes, those two subscriptions are not exactly bank-breakers at their lowest tiers, but in today’s economy what amounts to a few hundred dollars (all-in) can be noticeable. And that’s also not including if you are a UFC fan as those events are even more costly.

You can’t fault WWE or TKO for wanting to capitalize on its biggest experience of the year, but it does seem as if it’s reached a point of excess. WrestleMania will always be a “moment” for WWE and a moment of reflection on the past year for investors to take stock in the overall business. It just seems like this year, not all the gambling is being done by the casinos in Vegas.

data.title: Eutelsat Communications Could Win Or Lose In Equal Measure
data.description:
I am initiating coverage on French satellite operator Eutelsat Communications S.A. (OTCPK:EUTLF) in light of recent pricing volatility.

Eutelsat Communications primarily operates a fleet of legacy geostationary satellites as a service provider to other communication companies. Video is still the main source of revenue, with some mobility and fixed internet services supplementing. With the industry largely moving to low earth orbit satellite technology, Eutelsat merged with OneWeb in 2023 to build out a more competitive network.

EUTLF stock is essentially flat across the last year with significant volatility along the way, as shown below.

The company is in a very challenging position as the technology supporting the core business falls out of favor, but these revenue streams are critical to funding investments in a new low earth orbit, or LEO, network. Video also makes up more than 50% of revenue and continues to decline. The company is seeing traction in new business lines, but not yet enough to drive outsize growth for the overall business.

At the current run rate, with demand falling and new technology several years, and billions of investments away, I project a price target of $2.20. However, if Eutelsat can deliver on new businesses at current levels, there is more than 100% upside potential. With both scenarios likely until we get better information, I rate Eutelsat a hold.

Valuation

Eutelsat's most recent guidance spooked investors leading to some recent volatility. The biggest concern is capex being reduced, as this not only delays investments in new technology but calls into question the ability of the company to cash flow the needed investments.

I ran a discounted cash flow, or DCF, analysis of the business to set a price target. I made the following overall assumptions:

  • Capital and profitability growth at mid to low point of management guidance.
  • A discount rate of 8% based on an estimated WACC of 6% and hedging for current cash flow challenges.
  • Average revenue growth of 10% based on industry forecast.
  • Costs scale at 60% of revenue growth.

This analysis sets a price target of $2.2, down nearly 50% from current prices.

Demand For Core Product Is Fading

As more and more consumers cut the cord in favor of streaming, the traditional TV market has fallen below overall market growth and inflation in mature markets like North America and Europe.

Video still represents more than 50% of Eutelsat's revenue. Even double-digit growth in other businesses can't stem the losses with total business growing well behind the overall satellite market.

There also doesn't seem to be an end in sight as management expects the trend to continue for at least the rest of the year, if not longer.

It was also concerning to note that after a long run of a steady backlog, the backlog is starting to tick down. One quarter does not make a trend, but this is a watch-out moving forward.

Supply For New Product Is Rising

While there is plenty of room for Eutelsat to grow, I do not believe there is enough room to grow at a level that supports upside to the current price. The overall market is expected to grow at 10%. Above that rate, Eutelsat would have to take market share.

The challenge with taking market share is that there is tremendous competition in this space, including from providers that have the ability to both launch and operate on their owned assets (SpaceX and Amazon).

The largest and most aggressive competitor is Starlink from SpaceX (SPACE), and its revenue has risen rapidly as satellites come online.

Viasat, Inc. (VSAT) has been around longer, but revenue has picked up noticeably in 2024.

Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) is entering the space (pun intended) as well, and Amazon tends to win in most businesses they enter, especially with Jeff Bezos' Blue Origin a factor.

If the overall market is growing at 10%, but key competitors are growing at mid to high double-digits, it makes the task of growth even more challenging. Not to mention that the majority of revenue is still sitting on legacy equipment, while the competitors are rapidly moving to new equipment, and better capitalized overall.

Still A Chance For Outsized Returns

While the overall business is challenged, it is winning in specific areas like Fixed Connectivity. These areas benefit from 13-14% CAGRs through 2033.

The DCF analysis is highly sensitive to the revenue growth rate since costs scale rapidly. If the business can stabilize video revenue and grow connectivity services, while also funding capital needs, there is more than 100% upside well within the realm of possibilities. The only change made to the following DCF analysis was to increase revenue growth from 10% to 13% and allow costs to flow through.

Verdict

Eutelsat is at a challenging crossroads with the need to completely pivot the revenue base to new services and new technology. There is a significant upside for investors if this transition goes well, and a significant downside if it goes poorly. Price targets for both scenarios suggest 50% downside to 100% upside, leaving my overall recommendation on a hold. It is just too early to give up on the business, but we need to start seeing revenue growth and cash flow tick to the market.

Over the next several quarters I will be closely watching the business capital investment as well as signs of stabilization in video and improving growth in connectivity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: H&E Equipment: Special Situation With A 10% Yield
data.description:
Introduction

Since I sold a significant portion of my portfolio in late 2024, I have been looking for alternatives to allocate the funds.

The most obvious solutions have been U.S. Treasuries and high-yield corporate debt with short maturities, but some special situations also offer interesting returns.

In this article, I review the expected returns investors could get by holding H&E Equipment Services (NASDAQ:HEES) until the acquisition by Herc Holdings Inc. (NYSE:HRI) is completed.

Context

In August 2024, an informal conversation between executives of H&E and United Rentals (NYSE:URI) led to the idea of the acquisition.

At the time, H&E was trading at $48 per share (5x EBITDA).

After the initial suggestion by the United Rentals management that an “in the $80.00s” per share offer would be fair, the H&E management declined.

Negotiation went on until January 2025, when H&E accepted an offer for $92 per share.

However, the merger agreement included a 35-day go-shop period, which H&E used to contact 12 other companies seeking a higher bid. Two of them made an offer.

One of them was an offer from Herc Holdings, offering $78.75 per share plus 0.1287 shares of Herc per H&E share.

At the time, the offer represented a 14% premium to the United Rentals offer, which led management to accept it.

United Rentals decided not to submit a higher bid, receiving a $63.5M termination fee and showing disciplined capital allocation, as I explained in my recent coverage of the company.

The Deal

While H&E had a straightforward cash offer with United Rentals, the Herc deal is a bit more complicated, as it includes the stock consideration.

At the time of the offer, Herc was trading at $200.70 per share.

The stock consideration was valued at $25.80 per share, which added to the $78.75 offered in cash, resulted in a total consideration of $104.60 per share.

However, Herc would issue shares to cover the stock consideration, diluting the share count by 16.4%. The market rapidly adjusted the stock price, causing a significant decline in the following days.

After the acquisition, H&E shareholders will own 14.1% of Herc.

As I detailed in my latest Herc article where I assigned it a sell rating, my estimated fair value for the company after the acquisition is $115 per share.

At this price, which Herc is already approaching, the share consideration would be valued at $14.80. Added to the cash consideration, the final acquisition price would stand at $93.55 per H&E share.

Timing and Likelihood of Closing

One of the key aspects to determining the expected returns investors could obtain from this special situation is the timing and likelihood of the transaction closing.

For the transaction to close, half of the H&E shares have to be tendered into the offer. On that front, I do not see any risks.

Management has unanimously recommended accepting the offer, and directors combined own 12.5% of the company.

The premium is substantial, and the price offered is way above the industry average, as shown in the chart below.

For those reasons, I do not expect many shareholders to refuse the offer.

Timing-wise, the transaction is expected to close by mid-2025, as stated by H&E's management.

However, it will depend on regulatory approvals.

The initial expiration date was April 15, 2025, but it has already been extended until the regulatory review is underway.

The outside date is November 24, 2025, although it can be extended to February 2026.

The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) is currently reviewing the application to assess the impact on market competition.

Although the standard waiting period is 30 days, it is not unusual to take longer for larger transactions.

While the closing date is uncertain, the probability of closing from a regulatory perspective is high.

First, United Rentals has already studied the transaction before. Considering that United Rentals' market share (15%) is much larger than Herc's (4%), the transaction shouldn't raise any antitrust concerns.

Looking at similar transactions, there are no reasons for this one to get blocked by the FTC. The total consideration is ~$3.4B, and the combined market share after the acquisition would be at 6%.

As shown in the image below, larger transactions (especially when adjusted for inflation) have been approved in the past.

In addition, the merger agreement includes the obligation for Herc to divest assets if required.

Finally, the time between the submission to the FTC and the outside date is enough to get regulatory clearance.

Expected Returns

The main factors to determine the expected returns are the transaction closing date and the price of Herc's stock.

Considering the current stock price of H&E ($90.70 per share), if the deal is closed when Herc's stock is trading below $93 per share, the returns would be negative.

For this to happen, Herc's stock would need to decline by 20%.

In the rest of the scenarios, returns are positive, but the annual yield is also impacted by the timing.

In the transaction closing months column, I am considering the last day of each month.

The longer it takes for the transaction to close, the lower the annual yield.

If at the closing date, Herc is trading at current prices, which is the fair price I estimated in my coverage of the company, the annual yield would range from 16.5% if the transaction is closed at the end of June to 4.5% if it happens in December.

In my view, I assign the highest probabilities in July and August, with an average expected annual rate of 10%.

The current yield might be enough to justify the volatility risk, considering the likelihood of the deal closing and the current stock price of Herc.

Risks

The main risk for the thesis is a significant decline in equity markets.

If the current trade war situation escalates, and we see yet again an increase in volatility, the price of Herc's stock will decline, reducing the expected returns for H&E shareholders.

The other risk I identify is the delay in getting regulatory approval in combination with an economic slowdown.

If the transaction is not completed by November (could be extended to February 24, 2026) and a tough economic environment puts Herc in a difficult financial situation, the company might withdraw the offer.

Bottom Line

As Herc's stock approaches its fair value of $115 per share, H&E shareholders can expect an annual rate of return of 10% if the transaction is closed during July or August.

If the closing date is earlier, as management expects, returns are even greater.

However, the reader might not consider the current gap as big enough to justify the uncertainty around Herc's price.

In that case, a lower entry price would provide a higher margin of safety.

data.title: Farmers & Merchants Bancorp: A Dividend King, Unique Farming Risk
data.description:
This earnings season has been absolutely pivotal as we look for signs from the regional banks that the economy is showing signs of cracking. So far, some banks have shown some early signs of concerns, with deposits and loan demand down. However, other banks have been enjoying growth. The issue at hand? Yes, it is the so-called tariff risk.

Now here is the thing. While there is little direct impact, if regional customers, particularly on the business loan side of the bank, take a hit to their business, it could hurt loan demand or the ability to repay. And if revenue is down, it could hit deposits.

On the flip side, those with primarily consumer lending have next to no risk. But, if we see the economy worsen, we could see consumer spending slow, also slowing loan demand. So far, we have not seen it yet.

With that said, one of the regional banks we follow is in a special situation here. And that is the fiscal health of our farmers. In the short run, the tariff risk and trade war is seemingly harming our farmers. Longer-term the idea is for them to benefit American farmers, manufacturers, etc., but short term, there are big shocks, given that internationally sourced machinery and goods are adding costs. And with nations retaliating, it could mean less demand for our farmers' goods. Thus, we want to hone in on Farmers & Merchants Bancorp (OTCQX:FMCB).

This is a small, niche-type regional bank, with nearly three dozen branches across the Central Valley and East Bay areas in California. It is a major bank lender to agriculture in the U.S. We do like very much that this bank has raised its dividend for 60 consecutive years, and has paid dividends for 89 consecutive years. We have and do like it for dividend growth investing, but near term it faces some unique risks. The bank just reported Q1 earnings, and in this column, we examine the critical metrics we follow for regional banks.

In our opinion, the key metrics are holding up well, and the stock is only down about 5% during the selloff. In Q4, Farmers & Merchants Bancorp saw pockets of strength. Growth in deposits was 5.9% from the sequential Q4 hitting $5.0 billion. However, there was a slight decrease in total loans of 2.57% compared to Q4 2024. Many other regionals we have covered have seen declines in one or the other, so this is not so dramatic, but loans are down 3% from a year ago. Something to keep an eye on.

In terms of the risks to farming, CEO Kent Steinwert stated in the release:

Gross loans and leases were $3.6 billion at the end of the first quarter, down 2.56% from December 31, 2024 due in part to some seasonality in agricultural lending and due to our continued conservative approach in underwriting given the inverted yield curve which continues to not price in duration risk for loans and leases beyond three years. Credit quality remains solid and we continue to work closely with our borrowers as they work through the current economic cycle, particularly in a few agricultural products adversely impacted by negative conditions in the export market. However, the recent tariffs coupled with the weakened US dollar may improve export market opportunities for these products and may provide financial relief to affected customers.

This is insightful and explains that some loan demand decline was seasonal, but the bank has also tightened lending standards. As mentioned in the open, farmers face unique risks here. The export market has been tough, but the CEO sees changes to the export market and tariffs, as well as a weaker dollar helping farmers.

We also like that Farmers and Merchants expanded their margins. For Q1 2025 the net interest margin increased to 4.20% compared to 4.07% in the sequential Q4 2024 driven by a decrease in the average cost of total deposits. This drove net interest income to $53.1 million up $1 million from the sequential quarter. On the earnings front, this bank is incredibly profitable. Net income was $23.0 million, or $32.88 per share. This EPS was up 7.6% from a year ago and was a record Q1 for the company. For all of 2025, we are projecting $135 in EPS, putting the stock at just 7.5X FWD EPS. This is a very attractive price in our opinion.

The company has strong returns and asset quality. The return on average assets was 1.70%, up from 1.62 in the sequential Q4. Further, the return on average equity was 15.65% in the quarter. This was a 35 basis point improvement from Q4. The company also paid record dividends. Oh, and it is one of the most efficient banks. The efficiency ratio remains incredibly strong at 43.86%, also among the top in the industry. Very few regional banks have a sub-50% efficiency. While the credit quality remained incredibly strong, the allowance for credit losses was 2.10%, ticking up 6 basis points from Q4. There was a minor $300,000 provision for credit losses recorded in Q1, while non-accrual loans are just $193,000, while net charge-offs were $160,000 in the quarter.

Final thoughts

As of right now, the bank stock remains in a stable trading range. This is fine considering it is a dividend king given its history of dividend hikes. We continue to expect annual dividend hikes. We like the margins here, the income power, and the exceptional asset quality and efficiency.

While the bank does cater to agricultural customers which are under some short-term pressure, we are not seeing any major signs of trouble here yet. And, a weaker dollar could be a benefit to export demand.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Powell Has Backed Trump Into A Corner With The Market In The Crosshairs
data.description:
President Trump will wage his trade war without the Fed's help, and the stock market is likely to be the collateral damage. It appears that Fed Chair Jerome Powell's latest messaging is that the Fed is determined to prevent one-time price increases in the economy due to tariffs from becoming more persistent.

That said, Powell seems more than happy to let the stock market struggle and the economy slow down if Trump's tariffs are pushing up inflation rates. This will leave the weight of the trade war and the stock market's struggles all on President Trump and his trade policy.

Powell Would Be Justified In Not Cutting

There is little doubt that tariffs will cause a spike in inflation, especially given the size and scope of the tariffs currently proposed. It is likely to slow growth considerably over the coming year, and could even create a stagflation-type environment.

The market seems to think that the Fed will still cut the rate three times in 2025, based on December Fed Fund Futures trading at 3.5%. However, there is a good chance that if inflation starts to pick up again, the Fed will deliver no rate cuts in 2025, even if growth slows.

Much of the preliminary data suggests that inflation is accelerating and has continued in April. The Philly Fed and the Empire State prices paid both continued to rise, reaching levels not seen since the summer of 2022. While it has not appeared in the official CPI and PPI numbers yet, it would be surprising if it did not show up sometime later, as the CPI and PPI typically lag.

Inflation swaps are also pricing higher inflation rates, at least over the next one to two years. A 1-year inflation swap is trading at 3.15%, and a 2-year swap is trading at 2.7%. Both are up considerably since bottoming in early September 2024. If the predictive values of these market-based inflation expectations and price paid indexes are correct, the CPI and PPI are at a low point and are likely to rise significantly.

This will give Powell the ammunition needed not to cut rates even if the unemployment rate is rising, and economic growth is slowing. The latest summary of economic projections showed an estimated unemployment rate of 4.4% this year. Meanwhile, John Williams, the President of the NY Fed, noted that the unemployment rate could rise to between 4.5% and 5%, from its current 4.2%.

No Fed Put

If the Fed holds steady and doesn't cut rates, the focus will be squarely on Trump's policies, and it will be hard to deny that the tariffs and trade policies are causing the economic pain.

It also probably means the recent decline in the stock market will continue to, with the Fed not coming to the rescue. In a Q&A session on April 16, Chairman Jay Powell was explicitly asked about how the Fed put would be used, and the answer was no.

Powell's response should not have been a surprise, as the Fed's job is not to rescue the stock market every time it falls by a significant amount, such as 20 or 30%. It certainly was not the case in 2022, and it most likely won't be the case in 2025, either.

The key role of the Fed in the market is to ensure that markets are functioning adequately. When market functioning becomes disorderly, that is when the Fed is likely to step in and provide liquidity to the market if needed.

Currently, the market is functioning just fine and doesn't appear to be exhibiting much stress, at least since Trump put a 90-day pause on his proposed tariffs.

The message from Powell is clear: the Fed won’t be running to the rescue to buffer the blow from Trump’s trade war. Without monetary support, any market fallout will land squarely on the shoulders of the Trump administration.

There will be no Fed put this time.
